data.title: Similarweb: Well Positioned For Growth Acceleration In The Coming Quarters
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Similarweb (SMWB) previously, as the Q2 results were bullish, showing broad-based strength, better upmarket traction, and positive developments with monetizing its Gen-AI efforts. SWMB is scaling well with its Gen-AI strategy, and the sales ramp (which was the biggest overhang) is now turning into a tailwind. With RPO growth far outpacing revenue, it indicates an acceleration ahead. To me, the setup remains attractive, especially with the stock still priced like a low-teens grower.

GenAI

SMWB’s Gen-AI monetization strategy continues to be my emphasis as the pace of adoption is superb, which supports the idea that SMWB’s data is becoming an increasing input for the current AI era. I cited renewals and expansion of a major tech client as an example for adoption previously, but there is an even clearer example this time around. The Gen-AI Intelligence suite, which was only launched in April, has already seen annual recurring revenue (ARR) cross $1 million. Of course, this is still a very small part of the blended ARR, but the signal is clear. It tells us that enterprises are willing to pay for proprietary and hard-to-source data to fuel their AI strategy.

The way I see it, these customers are not really just buying generic data feeds. What they are really buying is the insights on how AI-driven traffic behaves (based on SMWB analytics). In layman's terms, customers (or brands) are afraid of losing visibility as consumers shift from Google search to AI search (e.g., ChatGPT prompts). They need to know if an AI chatbot recommends their product when a user asks a question. SMWB helps in this aspect in that it answers questions like "How many consumers are asking AI models about my brand?" and "What is the sentiment of the answer provided?"

The September launch of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) server should further strengthen SMWB’s position in this AI era. This MCP server is basically like an adapter. Instead of forcing a developer to build complex APIs to pull data, MCP allows AI agents (like Claude, Copilot, or OpenAI’s Agent Builder) to talk directly to the SMWB database and pull live data instantly. This doesn’t just make the lives of clients easier but ensures that SMWB continues to stay relevant as it gets embedded into AI workflows.

GTM Investments

SMWB is finally seeing the benefits of its hiring ramp, and I think this removes one of the biggest bear arguments. The main concern previously revolved around the idea that adding reps would drive up costs without the associated growth. The data points from the recent Q3 results should put that concern to rest, as management noted they now have 30% more sellers than a year ago, and productivity is improving very quickly. Yield per rep grew 28% sequentially, while deals per rep grew 21% in the same period. Basically, reps are getting more efficient, and that efficiency is translating into actual sales booked. Multiply this by the entire cohort of ramped-up hires, and I believe growth should accelerate from here.

One interesting data point to note is also the upgraded EBIT guidance. This tells me that underlying unit economics are improving so much that despite the 30% growth in sales force, management expects higher EBIT performance. Putting everything together, SMWB has gone through the phase of ramped-up hiring; this cohort of hires has shown solid improvements in efficiency with better underlying unit economics. As they continue to move up the maturity curve and SMWB laps the previous year's tough comp, we should see EBIT growth accelerate faster than revenue in the coming quarters.

Forward Indicators Signal a Coiled Spring

With the GTM investment overhang out of the way, the next concern in line, which I think is the main reason why the stock hasn’t reacted as I expected, is the low-teens revenue growth pace. To be fair, if revenue growth continues to stay in this range, it would go against what I have modelled, making the upside a lot less attractive. But I don’t think revenue growth is the right metric to focus on here. As I said before, the better indicator is remaining performance obligation (RPO), which points to a much stronger trajectory. For reference, in Q3, RPO grew 26% y/y to $268 million, which is significantly faster than the 11% revenue growth. The relative gap has widened from 9% in Q2 to 15% this recent quarter.

Based on my numbers, we should see growth accelerate in the next quarter if SMWB hits management’s guidance. The FY25 guide calls for $285–$288 million in revenue, which implies a Q4 2025 growth rate to be ~17% y/y at the midpoint. I have a pretty confident view that SMWB is going to at least meet this guide, as these are orders in the backlog (not new orders). This implied guide also tells us that RPO is indeed translating into revenue growth. It is only a matter of time before we see a more prominent impact. For what it's worth, management expects to recognize 68% of RPO over the next 12 months.

Valuation

My belief is that the market is still pricing SMWB as a low-to-mid-teens revenue grower, but the leading indicator (RPO) continues to point to >20% growth. As such, I stand by my view that growth will eventually accelerate and continue to model that. I still expect revenue growth to accelerate to be in line with RPO growth over the next two years, driving revenue to ~$430 million (slightly lower than my previous model, as I adjusted FY25 revenue growth to 15%, from 17%, to match management’s guide). I have laid out my case as to why SMWB deserves a higher revenue multiple, and I will quote it for reference:

I still model 3x forward revenue, as I believe an acceleration should easily push multiples back to the +1 standard deviation level. For what it’s worth, SMWB traded as high as 4.5x forward revenue in January, so 3x is not a high bar either.

At a 3x forward revenue multiple, I have a share price target of ~$16.

Investment Risk

The near-term risk is how lumpy LLM data revenue can be. A lot of these projects start as one-off evaluations, so unless they convert into recurring contracts, the sequential growth profile may be choppy. Management also noted that larger deals are taking longer to get signed, which suggests that customers are still cautious about budget, and this could delay the timeline for growth acceleration. The average revenue per user (ARPU) trend is also something to monitor. It has dipped to ~$48k in Q3, and while that reflects strong mass-market adoption, the mix shift could pressure unit economics if it gets worse.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating on SMWB. The Gen-AI investments continue to work, sales reps are becoming more productive, and the backlog signals stronger growth going into FY2026. Specifically, RPO strength and improving unit economics point to a return to >20% growth. With shares still trading at a cheap level relative to this outlook, I think the risk-reward still skews to the upside.

data.title: FRDM: Capturing New Trends In Emerging Markets
data.description:
Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF Overview

Emerging market equities have had a breakthrough year in 2025, following decades of underperformance. EM equities are up circa 30% YTD following lacklustre performance since 2011.

Of course, not all strategies are equal within emerging markets. Active vehicles like the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF (FRDM) have been able to deploy unique strategies to trounce the broader market. FRDM has outperformed the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM) by nearly 30 percentage points in the past three years.

The Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF has been a consistent outperformer and broke out even stronger this year. To make matters even better, this ETF still has compelling valuations and avoids some larger and overvalued emerging market constituents.

Consequently, the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF still looks like an excellent buy as 2026 approaches, even after its stellar 2025 run. This ETF has many unique characteristics that can help shield it from broader emerging market risks and is poised to continue outperforming.

Emerging Market Opportunities

Emerging markets have had a breakthrough year in 2025. This ETF has made concentrated bets on some winners, such as South Korea, and also maintains an overweight position in other markets that trade at a deep discount to the MSCI Emerging Markets Index. This unique performance will continue to produce uncorrelated returns.

While emerging markets have been underperforming for decades, 2025 represented a massive shift where EMs began to outperform the S&P 500 again, as they did in the late 80s and early 90s.

While MSCI Emerging Markets are not significantly cheap, trading at circa 17x earnings, many of the countries that FRDM invests in trade at compelling valuations. Only a handful of FRDM's top countries, including Taiwan and South Africa, trade at a premium to the MSCI EM Index.

This unique approach provides a margin of safety and has led to outperformance in recent years. Any massive pullbacks in markets like India and China would leave this fund largely unaffected, as it focuses on other Asian markets.

Emerging markets will likely have a 2 percentage point growth premium to developed markets, growing at 4%, while many of the countries this ETF invests in will grow at a much faster rate. This growth premium can allow emerging markets to continue to attract capital in 2026, especially since inflation risks and trade war risks have abated this year.

Emerging Market Risks to Note

Emerging markets have had a very bumpy ride since 2020, and there are plenty of risks to watch out for in 2026.

History has proven that large EM constituents can turn into basket cases. Argentina plunged nearly 50% in 2018 before being downgraded. US investors who chose to invest in Russia in the early 2020s because of the market's favorable valuation were wiped out.

The most important lesson to learn is that benchmarking isn't efficient. China, the largest MSCI EM constituent, has drastically underperformed in the past five years. EM investors have to think outside the box to avoid being forced to make overweight bets on underperformers.

ETFs like the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF help to change the game by avoiding popular indices and instead focusing on its own qualitative and quantitative criteria. Apart from avoiding dominant EM constituents, this ETF also has the freedom to implement other strategies, such as avoiding state-owned enterprises.

With these measures in place, it is still crucial to note that there are common issues that will impact all markets and that broader sell-offs in larger MSCI constituents could also have a spillover on smaller markets, even if they don't impact their respective economies.

Some main risks in emerging markets that are on my radar include the following:

  • Many emerging markets are at an inflection point where they are considering aggressive rate cuts. However, the combination of tariffs, geopolitical risks, and gyrations in the commodities markets could cause Central Banks to be cautious if inflation starts to bounce back, as it has in some markets in recent months.
  • Rising public debt is also another key issue. The IMF projects that EM public debt levels will rise from 73.9% to 83.8% by 2030.
  • Tensions between China and Taiwan have escalated in recent days due to Japan's involvement in the conflict. China and Taiwan collectively account for nearly half of the MSCI EM Index, so this could have a nasty spillover effect on emerging markets.
  • Even though EM equities have outperformed the S&P 500 amid trade war risks, there is still a risk that many export-driven markets could experience shocks in the coming quarters.

FRDM ETF: Unique Solutions for Investors

The Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF stands out based on its country selection, stock criteria, and other factors. One of its main differentiators is that it focuses on countries with higher economic freedom rankings. This is a unique characteristic that has led to clear, long-term outperformance.

It also focuses on other country-specific factors and liquidity requirements and chooses to avoid investing in SOEs. It invests in around 120 companies that fit this criteria and charges a relatively lower management fee of 0.49%.

As a result, the ETF's country composition is very unique. Many of its top countries by weight trade at a massive discount to the MSCI EM Index, and as a result, this ETF still trades at a compelling valuation following its stellar bull run this year.

For example, markets like South Korea, Chile, Poland, Brazil, Malaysia, and Indonesia are an absolute bargain now. Even markets like South Korea, which has been the top-performing market, trade in line with EM equities.

PE PB
MSCI South Korea 17.12 1.84
MSCI Chile 14.80 1.73
MSCI Poland 13.13 1.65
MSCI Brazil 9.44 1.71
MSCI Malaysia 15.12 1.52
MSCI Indonesia 14.81 2.36
Group average 14.07 1.80
MSCI EM Index 17.11 2.22

These markets still trade at a massive discount to the MSCI Em Index, which is dominated by larger players like China and India. This unique country approach is an excellent way to access outperforming markets.

Risks and Final Thoughts

The main risk with this ETF is that it may experience a short-term pullback due to tensions in emerging markets. All the risks I mentioned, including inflation, geopolitical risks, and rising public debt, could have a strong impact on this ETF.

The ETF's top 10 holdings still account for around 45% of its total assets, so the concentration risk of this ETF is high despite its country diversification. Some companies in its top 5 holdings are common names among EM funds.

This ETF is also heavily exposed to Taiwan and South Korea, markets that are very cyclical due to their dependence on electronics exports. While South Korea trades in line with EM equities, Taiwan trades at a premium after its recovery in recent years, so both markets could experience pullbacks based on their historical valuations.

Overall, the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF has demonstrated that its unique approach to these markets has led to outperformance, and I expect this trend to continue.

data.title: Noah Holdings: Positive About Q3 Earnings Surge And Financial Improvement Potential
data.description:
I'm sticking to a 'buy' rating for Noah Holdings (NOAH) (6686.HK).

3Q25 net profit jumped by over 50 percent. The '26 outlook for NOAH is promising.

Its growth drivers relating to digital assets and non-domestic markets were detailed in the prior September 2 write-up.

Multiple Factors Contributed To Strong Q3 Showing

NOAH's Jul-Sep 2025 numbers were revealed in a 6-K filing issued on Tue, Nov 25.

There are no quarterly consensus projects available for this name.

The firm's 3Q25 normalized bottom-line amounting to CNY 0.23B represented a +52% surge.

Its outperformance for the latest three-month timeframe was attributable to a number of reasons.

A thing to watch is NOAH's ventures outside of its home market, the "People's Republic of China" or "PRC."

My Sep 2 update cited the enterprise's 2Q25 call remarks with regard to "proactively entering mature financial markets such as the United States."

The proportion of its companywide turnover derived from non-China operations rose from 2Q25's 47% to 3Q2025's 49%.

NOAH's Q3 media release drew attention to "the establishment of its fourth booking center in the US during the quarter after obtaining a broker-dealer license there." That paid off with a +13% increase in "overseas registered clients."

Also, the financial environment has become healthier, which is positive for wealth managers.

At the earnings briefing, it noted that "we've been seeing some exits in the recent years" and can realize "valuation gains for some of the investments" in "the good market." The company saw its recent quarterly investment income grow +26% YoY to CNY 21M.

Separately, NOAH has been very focused on remaining lean.

3Q25 operational expenses were -2% lower as compared to that for the preceding quarter. In the same period, the group's top-line still went up +1% despite cost-cutting efforts.

Its press release highlighted a "6.5% year-over-year reduction in total (9M25) operating expenses", which reflects a "disciplined focus on operational efficiency."

I think NOAH's results will continue to get better, as outlined in the subsequent section.

Recurring Fee Growth And Streamlined Client Coverage Team Point To A Bright Future

The consensus anticipates that NOAH will turn around from a -1.9% sales decline (local currency terms) in FY25 to record a +4.2% topline expansion next year, according to S&P Capital IQ.

The sell-side also sees the company's FY2026 non-GAAP EPS rising by +8.7%. That compares favorably with its three consecutive years of contraction during the FY22-24 period.

There are two favorable read-throughs from NOAH's latest disclosures that have made me optimistic about its prospects.

One promising sign is the +5% year-on-year and +4% sequential increase in the firm's recurrent revenue to CNY 0.42B in 3Q25. This income stream accounted for two-thirds of its group-level turnover.

Its Q3 results call commentary mentioned that "the (recurrent revenue) increase was supported by higher overseas product management fee contribution." Management also stressed that "we're proactively managing our portfolio and strengthening overseas product fee contributions" going forward.

In the preceding section, I've already touched on NOAH's initiatives to broaden its footprint in a key international market, the U.S.

The downside risks pertaining to future performance could be substantially reduced. That assumes it maintains a high level of "repeating income" like that for 3Q, thanks to the strength associated with foreign operations.

On the other hand, NOAH's "number of overseas relationship managers" fell -11% QoQ and -7% YoY to "136" (source: 6-K filing) as of the end of September this year.

At first glance, it might seem that the group is pulling back on geographical diversification plans. That isn't true.

NOAH clarified at the 3rd quarter briefing that the drop in personnel numbers is driven by the "initial pilots" of "Artificial Intelligence Relationship Managers" or "AI RM" to "improve client outreach, content generation, and back-end operations." It explained further that "it's hard to rely on human beings (RMs)" as "the cost could be high."

There's huge potential for the firm to achieve structurally higher margins by utilizing AI.

My take is that the chances of NOAH achieving financial improvement in '26 are pretty good, taking into account tailwinds discussed here.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

Venturing abroad aggressively could mean that it is stepping outside its circle of competence. NOAH's actual results could be weaker-than-anticipated, if its wealth management solutions aren't well-received in certain new regions.

Its clients might be dissatisfied with the "AI RMs", which leads to a ramp-up in hiring again. The company's profitability in the coming quarters or years may fall short of expectations in that scenario.

The Final Word

I'm still a bull on this stock.

In my early-Sep piece, I listed "China International Capital Corporation (3908.HK), Futu Holdings (FUTU), and East Money (300059.SZ)" as its key peers. The next twelve months' normalized P/Es for these three names are 9.3x, 11.7x, and 31.5x, respectively.

NOAH's own forward earnings ratio is 7.0x, as per S&P Capital IQ. Its capital appreciation potential is at least +32.9% (9.3/7.0) using the cheapest of the lot, FUTU, as a comparison.

In my opinion, a return to positive top-line and bottom-line growth in FY26 should warrant a more demanding valuation for the company's shares.

data.title: How To Resolve The Housing Logjam
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Good morning! Here is the latest in trending:

More time off: Trading was halted at CME, impacting everything from forex and commodities to futures. Here's what happened.

On the move: Shares of German sportswear maker Puma (OTCPK:PMMAF) surged on word of a potential Chinese takeover.

Black Friday: A shooting in Washington D.C. led President Trump to "permanently pause migration from all Third World Countries."

New solutions

Interesting dynamics have played out in the housing market since the Federal Reserve began raising interest rates to counter inflation back in 2022. Some of those trends go back even further to the onset of the coronavirus pandemic, when the central bank dropped rates to zero at the same time people were seeking more space and lifestyle changes. The effects have led to an increasingly expensive environment for housing and rent, and triggered an affordability crisis across many markets.

Location, location, location? Higher borrowing costs steepened the price of new construction, while supply is short due to the many real estate investors who have clamored into the sector. Landlords have also raised rents to keep up with the inflationary environment, and many retiring baby boomers are still living in their homes rather than downsizing or moving to retirement communities. However, one of the biggest cost drivers is called the "lock-in effect," where people who locked into ultra-low interest rates before 2022 cannot move between markets or make traditional financial changes, which would increase liquidity and free up supply.

There have been many creative solutions to the current state of affairs, some of which are already common across the globe and are now being entertained by the Trump administration. One of them is mortgage assumability, where new owners of a house "assume" the mortgage terms of the seller (with full coverage of existing equity), and it already happens in the U.S. on a minimal scale with FHA and VA loans. Other touted remedies include mortgage portability, which permits buyers or sellers to simply "port" their existing mortgage terms to a new location, as well as the 50-year mortgages that made waves earlier this month.

Possible in America? According to the latest Wall Street Breakfast poll, Seeking Alpha readers think mortgage assumability and portability are a great way to ease the housing crisis, but not so much with 50-year debt. However, there are challenges to overcome. Land ownership in the U.S. is recorded and held at the local level, complicating efforts of porting a mortgage to a different state, or even to a different county. Another factor that also affects assumability is that the majority of mortgages are securitized and bundled with other sales, making it difficult to alter statements and terms, especially if things aren't mitigated by origination premiums, a higher percentage of variable rates, or larger down payments.
